blocks.x: Chapter 1 ended on a promise. 1.4 called two expressions equivalent when they agree at every value, and said the rules for rewriting one form into another would arrive in Chapter 2. This lesson delivers the first one, and it begins as a way to multiply ugly numbers in your head.
Compute \(9\cdot 204\) in your head. The number \(204\) splits into \(200+4\), and each piece is easy to multiply by \(9\). Multiply both pieces, then add. What do you get?
hints: Multiply the easy pieces, \(9\cdot 200\) and \(9\cdot 4\), then add the two results.
blocks.sol: Working \(9\cdot 204\) digit by digit gets there, but slowly. The split gives \(9\cdot 200=1800\) and \(9\cdot 4=36\), and \(1800+36=\boxed{1836}\).

blocks.x: The distributive property. For any numbers, \(a(b+c)=ab+ac\). The reason is one count taken two ways. Take \(a\) groups, each holding \(b+c\) items. Counting whole groups gives \(a(b+c)\) items, while counting the \(b\)s and the \(c\)s separately gives \(ab+ac\), and the totals must match because they count the same items. That is what the last problem did with \(9(200+4)\). The rules in 1.3 each concerned a single operation, and this is the first law connecting addition and multiplication to each other. As an instruction it reads, the outside factor multiplies each inside piece.
Compute \(6\cdot 398\) the same way, but treat \(398\) as \(400-2\). Multiply each piece by \(6\), then correct for the extra. What is the product?
hints: Round \(398\) up to \(400\), multiply, then pay back the \(6\cdot 2\) you borrowed.
blocks.sol: The pieces give \(6\cdot 400=2400\) and \(6\cdot 2=12\). Adding the \(12\) is the wrong turn, since \(398\) sits \(2\) below \(400\), so the correction comes off, \(2400-12=\boxed{2388}\).

blocks.x: Distribution over subtraction. The companion form \(a(b-c)=ab-ac\) is not a second law. By 1.3, subtracting \(c\) is adding \(-c\), so \(a(b-c)=a(b+(-c))=ab+a(-c)=ab-ac\), the same property applied to a sum. One sentence now covers both forms. The outside factor multiplies each inside piece, and each piece keeps the sign it came with. In \(6(400-2)\) the second piece is \(-2\), which is why the \(12\) was subtracted rather than added.

blocks.cap: Both bands show one fact. On top, a sheet's area is counted whole as \(7(x+4)\) or piece by piece as \(7x+28\), and the two counts measure the same sheet. Below, four rows of dots are counted row by row as \(4(10+2)\) or by color as \(40+8\), landing on \(48\) either way. One thing counted two ways must give one total, and that is the whole proof of \(a(b+c)=ab+ac\).

Nothing in the rule requires the inside pieces to be plain numbers. Expand \(7(n+6)\). The result has two terms, one with \(n\) and one without. Enter the constant term of the result, the term with no \(n\).
hints: The \(7\) multiplies both pieces inside, the \(n\) and the \(6\), not just the first.
blocks.sol: Writing \(7n+6\), with the \(7\) multiplying only the first piece, is the classic mistake. Distributing to both pieces gives \(7\cdot n+7\cdot 6=7n+42\), and the term with no \(n\) is \(\boxed{42}\).

blocks.x: Expanding. Rewriting \(7(n+6)\) as \(7n+42\) is called expanding, and the outside factor multiplies every term inside. The result is not merely similar, it is equivalent, equal at every value of \(n\). That settles a debt from 1.4, where \(6(t+2)\) and \(6t+12\) matched at one input, a good sign but not proof. One application of the distributive property now proves it, \(6(t+2)=6t+12\) at every \(t\). Checking values can disprove, distribution can prove. One reading note, an expanded result has a coefficient and a constant term, so enter the part the question names.
Expand \(5(3w-4)\). The first inside piece already carries a coefficient, and the second carries a minus sign. Enter the coefficient of \(w\) in the result.
hints: The minus belongs to the \(4\). Multiply \(5\) by \(3w\), then \(5\) by \(-4\), keeping that sign.
blocks.sol: Two wrong turns are on offer, entering the constant \(-20\) instead of the coefficient, and dropping the minus to get \(+20\). Distributing gives \(5\cdot 3w=15w\) and \(5\cdot(-4)=-20\), so the result is \(15w-20\) and the coefficient of \(w\) is \(\boxed{15}\).

blocks.x: Expand \(g(g+11)\). The outside factor is now the variable itself, and 1.5 already settled what \(g\cdot g\) is. Enter the coefficient of the plain-\(g\) term of the result, not the \(g^2\) term.
hints: By 1.5, \(g\cdot g=g^2\). The other product is \(g\cdot 11\).
blocks.sol: Distributing gives \(g\cdot g+g\cdot 11=g^2+11g\). Entering \(1\), the coefficient of \(g^2\), answers the wrong part of the result. The plain-\(g\) term is \(11g\), so its coefficient is \(\boxed{11}\).

blocks.x: The outside factor can be a variable. The rule holds for any numbers, and a variable stands for a number, so the factor outside the parentheses can be a letter, \(g(g+11)=g\cdot g+g\cdot 11=g^2+11g\). Each product follows rules already in hand, \(g\cdot g=g^2\) by 1.5's count of factors, and \(g\cdot 11\) is written \(11g\) with the coefficient first. Every term inside still gets multiplied. Nothing in the rule depends on what the outside factor is, a number, a letter, or a larger expression built from both.
A student expands \(8(y+3)\) and writes \(8y+3\), multiplying only the first term inside. Evaluate both expressions at \(y=2\), each on its own. How far apart are their values?
hints: Substitute \(y=2\) into \(8(y+3)\) and into \(8y+3\) separately, then compare the two numbers.
blocks.sol: At \(y=2\), the original is \(8(2+3)=8\cdot 5=40\), while the student's version is \(8\cdot 2+3=19\). Entering \(40\) or \(19\) alone answers a different question, the ask is how far apart they sit. By 1.4, one disagreement is all it takes, and the gap is \(40-19=\boxed{21}\).

blocks.x: Partial distribution. The most common expanding error is multiplying only the first term, claiming \(a(b+c)=ab+c\). That claim is false, and one evaluation at any convenient value exposes it, the same checking move 1.6 practiced. One input, two different outputs, and the claim fails. The fix is mechanical. A factor sitting against parentheses multiplies every term inside, every time, however many terms there are.

A bare minus sign directly in front of parentheses means multiplication by \(-1\), so \(-(4d-7)\) means \((-1)(4d-7)\). Rewrite it by distributing that \(-1\) over both inside terms. Enter the constant term of the result.
hints: Both inside terms get multiplied by \(-1\), so both signs flip, not just the first.
blocks.sol: Flipping only the first sign gives \(-4d-7\), the standard error. Distributing hits both terms, \((-1)(4d)=-4d\) and \((-1)(-7)=+7\), so the result is \(-4d+7\) and the constant term is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: Distributing \(-1\). A bare minus in front of parentheses is multiplication by \(-1\), so every sign inside flips, positive terms turning negative and negative terms turning positive. Nothing new is involved, this is the distributive property with \(a=-1\), and since the product of two negatives is positive, the \(-7\) inside came out as \(+7\). Make the full flip a habit now. You will use it constantly when 2.3 subtracts whole expressions, and flipping only the first sign is among the most common sign errors in all of algebra.
The rule works for any number of inside terms. Compute \(12\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}\right)\) by distributing the \(12\) over all three terms. What value comes out?
hints: Each product, \(12\cdot\frac{1}{2}\), \(12\cdot\frac{1}{3}\), and \(12\cdot\frac{1}{4}\), is a whole number.
blocks.sol: Adding the fractions first gives \(\frac{13}{12}\), and \(12\cdot\frac{13}{12}=13\) works, at the cost of a common denominator. Distributing skips all of that, \(12\cdot\frac{1}{2}+12\cdot\frac{1}{3}+12\cdot\frac{1}{4}=6+4+3=\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: A number trick. Think of a number, add \(5\), triple the result, subtract \(15\), then divide by \(3\). Start with \(47\) and follow the four steps in order. What number comes out?
hints: Follow the four steps one at a time, keeping each intermediate result.
When you finish, compare the number that came out with the number that went in.
blocks.sol: The steps take \(47\) to \(52\) to \(156\) to \(141\) and back to \(47\). A common slip is to subtract \(15\) before tripling, which turns \(52\) into \(37\) and ends at \(37\), so keep the steps in the stated order. Writing the start as \(n\), the first three steps build \(3(n+5)-15\), which expands to \(3n+15-15=3n\), and dividing by \(3\) leaves \(n\). Whatever number goes in comes back out, so the result is \(\boxed{47}\).

blocks.x: When two parenthesized expressions multiply each other, as in \((x+2)(x+3)\), the distributive property simply runs twice, once for each term of the first factor. That door opens with quadratics in Chapter 9.

The first rewriting tool is in hand. Ugly products split into easy pieces, and parentheses open without changing an expression's value at any input, which is exactly what 1.4 meant by equivalent. Next, 2.2 puts evaluation to work on heavier expressions, and in 2.3 this rule runs in reverse, reading \(ab+ac\) as \(a(b+c)\), a move called factoring.

review.x: Mental math first. Compute \(8\cdot 306\) in your head by splitting \(306\) into \(300+6\) and multiplying each piece by \(8\). Enter the product.
hints: \(8\cdot 300\) and \(8\cdot 6\) are both easy. Add the two results.
review.sol: A common slip is to lose the middle zero and compute \(8\cdot 36=288\), which is way too small for eight copies of a number near \(300\). Keep the pieces as \(300\) and \(6\). Then \(8\cdot 300=2400\) and \(8\cdot 6=48\), so \(8\cdot 306=2400+48=\boxed{2448}\).

review.x: Compute \(4\cdot 997\) by treating \(997\) as \(1000-3\). Multiply each piece by \(4\), then correct for the overshoot. Enter the product.
hints: The product \(4\cdot 1000\) overshoots, and \(4\cdot 3\) measures by how much.
review.sol: Over subtraction the rule reads \(4(1000-3)=4\cdot 1000-4\cdot 3\). Adding the correction instead of subtracting it gives \(4012\), the wrong direction, since \(997\) sits below \(1000\). The product is \(4000-12=\boxed{3988}\).

review.x: Expand \(9(u+7)\) by multiplying each inside term by \(9\). Enter the constant term of the result, the term with no \(u\).
hints: The \(9\) multiplies both pieces inside the parentheses, not just the \(u\).
review.sol: Multiplying only the first term gives \(9u+7\), the partial-distribution error, and \(9u+7\) is not equivalent to \(9(u+7)\). The \(9\) multiplies the \(7\) as well, so the expansion is \(9u+63\) and the constant term is \(\boxed{63}\).

review.x: Expand \(6(5h-9)\), keeping each inside term's sign as you distribute. Enter the coefficient of \(h\) in the result, the number multiplying it.
hints: The minus belongs to the \(9\), so the second product comes out negative.
review.sol: Both products keep their signs, \(6\cdot 5h=30h\) and \(6\cdot(-9)=-54\), so the expansion is \(30h-54\). Entering \(-54\) answers the wrong part, and writing \(+54\) loses the sign. The coefficient of \(h\) is \(\boxed{30}\).

review.x: Expand \(t(t+17)\). The outside factor is a variable this time. Enter the coefficient of the plain-\(t\) term of the result, not the \(t^2\) term.
hints: The outside factor multiplies both terms inside. One product is \(t\) times \(t\), and the other product is the one that makes the plain-\(t\) term.
review.sol: The outside factor multiplies both terms, \(t\cdot t=t^2\) and \(t\cdot 17=17t\), so the expansion is \(t^2+17t\). It is tempting to answer with the coefficient of \(t^2\), which is \(1\), or to type the whole term \(17t\), but the question asks only for the coefficient of the plain-\(t\) term. That term is \(17t\), so the answer is \(\boxed{17}\).

review.x: Rewrite \(-(6p-19)\) by distributing the minus. A bare minus in front of parentheses means multiplication by \(-1\). Enter the constant term of the result.
hints: The bare minus means multiply by \(-1\), so both inside signs flip.
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign gives \(-6p-19\), the usual error. Multiplying each term by \(-1\) gives \(-6p\) and \((-1)(-19)=+19\), so the result is \(-6p+19\) and the constant term is \(\boxed{19}\).

review.x: A student claims that \(5(x+8)=5x+8\). Settle it the 1.4 way, evaluate both expressions at \(x=3\). How far apart are their values?
hints: Substitute \(3\) into each expression separately, the grouped one inside first.
review.sol: At \(x=3\), \(5(x+8)=5\cdot 11=55\) while \(5x+8=15+8=23\). Entering \(55\) or \(23\) alone answers a different question, the ask is the gap between them. One disagreement proves the claim false, and the gap is \(55-23=\boxed{32}\).

review.x: Three terms and two letters this time. Expand \(5(2a+9b-7)\), multiplying every term by \(5\). Enter the coefficient of \(a\).
hints: Three terms inside means three products, and only one of them involves \(a\).
review.sol: The \(5\) multiplies all three terms, giving \(10a+45b-35\). The ask names the coefficient of \(a\), so \(45\) and \(-35\) answer the wrong part. It is \(5\cdot 2=\boxed{10}\).

review.x: Compute \(15\left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{5}\right)\) by distributing the \(15\) over both fractions. Done this way, no common denominator is needed.
hints: \(15\) times each fraction comes out whole.
review.sol: Adding the fractions first gives \(\frac{16}{15}\) and also works, but distributing skips the common denominator entirely. \(15\cdot\frac{2}{3}=10\) and \(15\cdot\frac{2}{5}=6\), so the value is \(10+6=\boxed{16}\).

review.x: Compute \(7\cdot 8.03\) by splitting \(8.03\) into \(8+0.03\). Decimals distribute the same way whole numbers do. Enter the exact product.
hints: Two products, \(7\cdot 8\) and \(7\cdot 0.03\), then add them.
review.sol: The split gives \(7\cdot 8=56\) and \(7\cdot 0.03=0.21\). Misplacing the decimal point to get \(2.1\) ruins the sum, so keep \(0.21\) and the product is \(56+0.21=\boxed{56.21}\).

review.x: Expand \(3(z^2+6z+9)\). The inside has three terms, one of them squared, so the result has three terms too. Enter the constant term.
hints: The \(3\) multiplies all three terms, the squared one included.
review.sol: Every term gets the factor, so the expansion is \(3z^2+18z+27\). Stopping after two terms and leaving the \(9\) untouched is the partial-distribution error again. The constant term is \(\boxed{27}\).

review.x: Whatever number \(k\) stands for, the expression \(4(k+6)-4k\) comes out to the same value every time. Enter that value.
hints: Distribute the \(4\) over \(k+6\) first.
After distributing, look at the two \(k\) terms side by side.
review.sol: The tempting slip is to distribute the \(4\) onto \(k\) only and write \(4k+6-4k\), which would leave \(6\). The \(4\) multiplies both terms inside the parentheses, so distributing gives \(4k+24-4k\). The \(4k\) and \(-4k\) cancel completely, leaving \(24\) no matter what \(k\) is. That is the same cancellation that forced the number trick's output earlier in the lesson. The value is \(\boxed{24}\).

blocks.x: Evaluating was settled in 1.6, so nothing about the method changes here, the expressions just get heavier. Substitute in parentheses, compute by the order of operations, read off one number. This lesson is six problems, and each one adds a step the last did not have.

Evaluate \(5-4y\) at \(y=-7\). There are two minus signs in the second term, one printed in the expression and one that comes with the value, so write the substitution with parentheses to keep them separate.
hints: Read \(4(-7)\) as one finished product before subtracting anything.
blocks.sol: The careless line reads \(5-28=-23\), which loses the value's sign. Substituted in parentheses, the term is \(4(-7)=-28\), and subtracting \(-28\) is adding \(28\), so \(5-4(-7)=5+28=\boxed{33}\).

blocks.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{3k}{4}+\dfrac{20}{k}\) at \(k=8\). The variable sits under a fraction bar this time, and it appears in two places at once. Enter the value as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Both \(k\)s become \(8\) at the same moment, one above a bar and one below, and each bar finishes as its own division.
blocks.sol: Combining across the bars into \(\frac{24+20}{4+8}=\frac{44}{12}\) merges two separate divisions and is wrong. Each bar finishes on its own, \(\frac{24}{4}=6\) and \(\frac{20}{8}=\frac{5}{2}\), so the value is \(6+\frac{5}{2}=\boxed{\dfrac{17}{2}}\).

blocks.x: Same habits, heavier loads. Every problem here runs on the routine from 1.6, substitute in parentheses at every appearance, then work by the order from 1.2, writing each intermediate value down. What changes is where the variable sits. A letter can stand anywhere a number can, including under a fraction bar, and the bar still groups its whole top and its whole bottom. Writing the middle values down costs a few seconds and catches most sign errors before they compound.
Evaluate \(\sqrt{-12w}\) at \(w=-3\). The minus printed under the bar looks like the even-root trouble from 1.5. Substitute first, then decide whether the root exists.
hints: Compute the number actually under the bar once \(w\) is replaced, then judge.
blocks.sol: Rejecting the root on sight is the tempting wrong turn, since the printed minus is not the value's sign. Substituting gives \(-12(-3)=36\) under the bar, and \(\sqrt{36}=\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: Judge after substituting. An even root needs a nonnegative value under its bar, the rule from 1.5, and the sign that matters is the sign of the value after substituting, never the sign printed in the expression. \(-12w\) opens with a minus on the page, yet it is positive whenever \(w\) is negative. This is the value-not-appearance idea from 1.1 working under a radical bar. Compute what sits under the bar first, and only then decide whether the root exists.

blocks.cap: On top, the grey route rejects \(\sqrt{-4q}\) at sight from the printed minus, which judges the expression instead of the value. Substituting \(q=-25\) puts \(-4(-25)=100\) under the bar, so the root exists and equals \(10\). Below, \((2u+3)^2\) at \(u=9\) runs inside out, the innermost \(u\) becomes \(9\), the parentheses finish as \(21\), and squaring last gives \(441\).

blocks.x: Evaluate \(n^2-5n+3\) at \(n=-2\). Three terms, three sign decisions, one value, and every appearance of \(n\) gets its own parentheses.
hints: Write the parentheses at both appearances of \(n\) before doing any arithmetic.
Subtracting \(5(-2)\) is subtracting a negative.
blocks.sol: Reading the square as \(-2^2=-4\) gives a total of \(9\), and dropping the middle term's sign gives \(4-10+3=-3\), both wrong. Correctly \((-2)^2=4\) and \(-5(-2)=+10\), so the value is \(4+10+3=\boxed{17}\).

blocks.x: Evaluate \((2h-3)^2+h\) at \(h=6\). The parentheses hold a whole expression this time. Build the inside value first, square the result, then finish the sum.
hints: The exponent applies to the whole parenthesized value, so finish \(2h-3\) before squaring anything.
blocks.sol: Squaring term by term gives \(144-9+6=141\), which is wrong because the exponent takes the finished inside value. That value is \(2(6)-3=9\), and \(9^2=81\), so the total is \(81+6=\boxed{87}\).

blocks.x: Inside out with variables. A compound expression evaluates as a chain of small evaluations, innermost pieces first, which is the inside-out rule from 1.2 applied to substituted values. In \((2h-3)^2+h\), the inside finished as \(9\), the \(9\) fed the square, and the \(81\) fed the final sum. Each intermediate number is the input to the next layer, so a long expression is never one giant computation, it is many short ones in a row.

This one uses every skill from the lesson. Evaluate \(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-12v}}{v}\) at \(v=-4\). The bar covers all of \(1-12v\), the input is negative, and a division finishes the job. Enter the value as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: Substitute before touching the root, so compute \(1-12(-4)\) under the bar first.
After taking the root, divide by \(v\) itself. Its value is \(-4\), sign included.
blocks.sol: Two mistakes are tempting here. Stopping at the radical because of the minus sign in \(1-12v\) is one, but \(1-12(-4)=1+48=49\), which is fine under the bar. Dropping the sign of the denominator is the other, and it gives \(\dfrac{7}{4}\), also wrong. The top is \(\sqrt{49}=7\), the bottom is \(-4\), and \(7\div(-4)=\boxed{-\dfrac{7}{4}}\).

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: A GPS receiver finds your distance from each satellite by evaluating a compound expression, squared differences feeding a square root, inside out, many times every second.

The expressions got heavier and the method never changed. Parentheses on every substitution, signs judged after substituting, computation run from the inside out. Next, 2.3 turns the distributive property around, reading \(ab+ac\) as \(a(b+c)\), a rewrite called factoring.

review.x: Evaluate \(9-2t\) at \(t=-3\).
hints: Substitute with parentheses, then finish the product \(2(-3)\) before subtracting it.
review.sol: Dropping the sign reads the second term as \(2\cdot 3\) and gives \(9-6=3\). With parentheses the substitution is \(9-2(-3)=9+6=\boxed{15}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{m}{3}+m\) at \(m=12\).
hints: Only the first \(m\) sits under the bar. The second one is a plain \(12\) added on.
review.sol: Pulling the lone \(m\) under the bar reads \(\frac{12+12}{3}=8\), but the bar groups only its own top. The first term is \(12\div 3=4\), and \(4+12=\boxed{16}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\sqrt{2-7d}\) at \(d=-2\). Substitute before judging the root.
hints: Find the number actually under the bar once \(d\) is replaced.
review.sol: Rejecting at sight because of the printed \(-7d\) is the tempting wrong turn, but the sign that matters is the value's after substituting. Since \(-7(-2)=14\), the bar holds \(2+14=16\), and \(\sqrt{16}=\boxed{4}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(w^2+3w\) at \(w=-18\).
hints: The square takes the whole value, sign included.
review.sol: Reading the square as \(-18^2=-324\) makes the total \(-378\). With parentheses, \((-18)^2=324\) and \(3(-18)=-54\), so the value is \(324-54=\boxed{270}\).

review.x: Evaluate \((3g-4)^2\) at \(g=5\).
hints: Finish the whole inside \(3g-4\) before squaring anything.
review.sol: Squaring \(g\) first reads \(3(25)-4=71\), but the exponent applies to the whole parenthesized value. Inside first, \(3(5)-4=11\), and \(11^2=\boxed{121}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{40}{p}-p\) at \(p=-5\).
hints: The divisor keeps its sign, and so does the subtracted \(p\).
review.sol: The tempting slip is to drop the negative in the division, read \(40\div 5=8\), and land on \(8+5=13\). The first term is really \(40\div(-5)=-8\), and subtracting \(p\) means subtracting \(-5\), so \(-8+5=\boxed{-3}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\sqrt{h^2+63}\) at \(h=-1\).
hints: \(h^2\) means \((-1)^2\). Judge its sign after substituting.
review.sol: Reading the square as \(-1^2=-1\) leaves \(62\) under the bar, which has no clean root. Correctly \((-1)^2=1\), so the bar holds \(1+63=64\), and \(\sqrt{64}=\boxed{8}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{a^2+b}{a+b}\) at \(a=-3\) and \(b=6\).
hints: Finish the whole top and the whole bottom before dividing.
review.sol: Reading \(a^2\) as \(-3^2=-9\) makes the top \(-3\) and the value \(-1\). With parentheses the top is \((-3)^2+6=15\) over a bottom of \(-3+6=3\), so the value is \(15\div 3=\boxed{5}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(2c^2-c^3\) at \(c=-3\).
hints: An odd power of a negative keeps the sign, an even power clears it.
review.sol: Treating the cube as \(+27\) gives \(18-27=-9\). The even power clears the sign, so \(2(-3)^2=18\), while the odd power keeps it, so \((-3)^3=-27\), and \(18-(-27)=18+27=\boxed{45}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{\sqrt{50-t^2}}{t+12}\) at \(t=-5\). Enter the value as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: \(t^2\) means \((-5)^2\). Settle what sits under the bar first.
Finish the whole top, then the whole bottom, and divide last.
review.sol: Reading \((-5)^2\) as \(-25\) puts \(75\) under the bar with no clean root, and reading the bottom as \(-5-12\) breaks the other half. The bar holds \(50-25=25\), so the top is \(5\), the bottom is \(-5+12=7\), and the value is \(\boxed{\dfrac{5}{7}}\).

blocks.x: Lesson 2.1 ended with a promise, and 2.2 repeated it. The distributive property turns \(a(b+c)\) into \(ab+ac\), and one day the rule would run backward. This is that day. Reading \(ab+ac\) as \(a(b+c)\) is called factoring, and before a single letter appears it is pure arithmetic muscle.
Compute \(57\cdot 69+57\cdot 31\) without working out either product. Both terms contain the factor \(57\), so pull it out front and see what the leftover sum becomes.
hints: The first term is \(57\) copies of \(69\), and the second is \(57\) more copies of \(31\). Count copies before multiplying anything.
blocks.sol: Grinding out both products works but wastes the structure. Both terms share the factor \(57\), so the whole sum is \(57\) copies of \(69+31\), and $$57\cdot 69+57\cdot 31=57(69+31)=57\cdot 100=\boxed{5700}.$$

blocks.x: Factoring. Reading \(ab+ac\) as \(a(b+c)\) is called factoring, the distributive property read right to left. Expanding opens parentheses, factoring builds them, one equality with two directions of travel. The rewrite is worth having because a shared factor collapses a sum of products into a single product, which is exactly what turned the last problem into a one-step computation, \(57\) copies of \(69\) plus \(57\) copies of \(31\) making \(57\) copies of \(100\).
Compute \(58\cdot 91-58\cdot 81\) the same way. The shared factor comes out front, and this time a subtraction is left behind it. What is the value of the whole expression?
hints: The first product is \(58\) copies of \(91\) and the second is \(58\) copies of \(81\), so subtracting leaves \(58\) copies of some difference.
blocks.sol: Working both products gives \(5278-4698=580\), the long way around. Pulling the \(58\) out front keeps the subtraction inside, $$58\cdot 91-58\cdot 81=58(91-81)=58\cdot 10=\boxed{580}.$$

blocks.x: The round trip. The subtraction form \(ab-ac=a(b-c)\) is not a second law, since 1.3 made subtracting \(c\) the same as adding \(-c\). More important is the habit this lesson runs on. Every proposed factoring gets checked by expanding it back, and 2.1 already made you fluent at expanding, so the answer key is always in your hands. The match must be exact, every term and every sign, or the factoring is wrong. The check matters most against the factoring twin of partial distribution, dividing only the first term by the shared factor, and one expansion exposes that slip immediately.

blocks.cap: The top band is this lesson's habit. \(3(y+8)\) and \(3y+24\) are the same equality read in opposite directions, expanding opens the parentheses and factoring rebuilds them, so any proposed factoring is checked by expanding it back and demanding an exact match. The bottom band is why factoring changes nothing. The same \(20\) tiles are counted loose as \(8+12\) or in four matching groups as \(4(2+3)\), regrouped but never changed.

Letters change nothing. The expression \(4w+36\) factors as \(4(w+c)\) for one constant \(c\). Divide each term by the shared \(4\), then enter \(c\).
hints: Both terms get divided by the \(4\), not just the first one.
blocks.sol: Writing \(4(w+36)\) divides only the first term, and expanding it gives \(4w+144\), not the original, so it fails the round trip. Dividing both terms gives \(4(w+9)\), and expanding lands exactly back on \(4w+36\), so \(c=\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: How to factor. Find a factor shared by every term, divide each term by it, and write the leftovers inside the parentheses. Each leftover keeps the sign of the term it came from, so a negative term leaves a negative leftover. The search runs on the parts vocabulary from 1.4, since what you scan is each term's factors, hunting for one that appears in every term. Then the round trip settles the result, expanding it must reproduce the original exactly.

Dana factors \(12h+30\) as \(2(6h+15)\), and expanding it back checks out. But inside the parentheses, \(6h\) and \(15\) still share a factor, so the job is unfinished. Enter the largest number that divides both \(12h\) and \(30\).
hints: What is the largest number that divides both \(12\) and \(30\)?
blocks.sol: Settling for the \(2\) is tempting since the round trip passed, but \(\gcd(12,30)=6\), and the complete form is \(6(2h+5)\), whose leftovers share nothing but \(1\). The largest common factor is \(\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: Factor completely. A partial factoring like \(2(6h+15)\) passes the round trip, since it really does equal \(12h+30\), so completeness needs its own check. Look at the leftovers inside the parentheses, and if they still share a factor bigger than \(1\), pull it out too. An expression is factored completely once the leftovers share nothing but \(1\). The number part of the largest common factor is the GCD from Prealgebra 3.5, which is why \(\gcd(12,30)=6\) finished the job in one pull as \(6(2h+5)\).
A letter can be the shared factor. Since \(y^2\) means \(y\cdot y\) (1.5), both terms of \(y^2+10y\) contain \(y\), so the expression factors as \(y(y+c)\). Enter \(c\).
hints: Divide each term by \(y\) and see what each one leaves behind.
blocks.sol: Stopping because no number bigger than \(1\) is shared misses that \(y\) itself is shared. Dividing each term by \(y\) leaves \(y+10\), and the round trip \(y(y+10)=y^2+10y\) confirms \(c=\boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: Number and letter combine. Enter the greatest common factor of the two terms of \(8t^2+20t\), typed like a term, for example \(6z\).
hints: Find the number part and the letter part separately, then put them together.
blocks.sol: Entering just \(4\) or just \(t\) stops halfway. The number part is \(\gcd(8,20)=4\), both terms share one \(t\), and the complete form is \(4t(2t+5)\), so the greatest common factor is \(\boxed{4t}\).

blocks.x: The full scan. The greatest common factor of a set of terms collects two things, the GCD of the number parts and every letter all the terms share. Pulling \(4t\) out of \(8t^2+20t\) leaves \(2t+5\), and since \(2t\) and \(5\) share nothing but \(1\), the factoring is complete. One expansion, \(4t(2t+5)=8t^2+20t\), confirms the whole thing in a line.
A student factors \(8n+36\) as \(4(2n+8)\). Test it the 1.4 way, evaluate the original and the claimed factoring at \(n=1\), each on its own. How far apart are their values?
hints: Work out each expression at \(n=1\) separately, then subtract the results.
blocks.sol: Trusting the factoring because \(4\) really does divide both \(8n\) and \(36\) is the trap, since the leftover constant is wrong. The original gives \(8+36=44\) while the claim gives \(4(2+8)=40\), a gap of \(4\). Expanding catches it faster, since \(4(2n+8)=8n+32\) rather than \(8n+36\), so the factoring is wrong either way. The gap is \(\boxed{4}\).

blocks.x: Nothing survives the check. A wrong factoring cannot pass the round trip, and by 1.4's one-mismatch rule it cannot even survive a single evaluation, since one input with two different outputs proves the claim false. The expansion check is stronger and usually faster, one distribution compared against the original, term by term and sign by sign. Check every factoring, every time, by expanding. It costs five seconds and catches everything.
Three terms this time. The expression \(9a+27b-18\) factors as \(9(a+3b+c)\), and each term keeps its sign as the \(9\) comes out. Enter \(c\), sign included.
hints: What is \(-18\) divided by \(9\), sign and all?
blocks.sol: Dropping the minus and entering \(2\) is the trap. The third term is \(-18\), and \(-18\div 9=-2\), so the round trip \(9(a+3b-2)=9a+27b-18\) confirms \(c=\boxed{-2}\).

blocks.x: In \(m(m-6)+8(m-6)\), both terms contain the whole factor \(m-6\). Pull the chunk out front exactly the way the \(57\) came out in this lesson's first problem. The result is \((m+a)(m-6)\). Enter \(a\).
hints: Treat \(m-6\) as a single object, like the shared \(57\) in the hook problem.
The first term is \(m\) copies of \(m-6\), and the second term adds some more copies of the same thing. Count the total number of copies.
blocks.sol: It is tempting to expand \(m(m-6)\) and \(8(m-6)\) into four separate terms first, but that erases the shared chunk and leaves nothing to pull out. Instead treat \(m-6\) as one object. The first term is \(m\) copies of it and the second is \(8\) copies of it, which is \(m+8\) copies in all, so the expression equals \((m+8)(m-6)\). Distributing \(m-6\) back over \(m+8\) reproduces the original in a single step, and chapter 9 factors quadratics with exactly this chunk move, so \(a=\boxed{8}\).

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: The padlock in your browser's address bar rests on factoring being hard. Multiplying two enormous primes takes a computer an instant, but recovering them from the product alone would outlast the age of the universe.

Factoring reads distribution backward, the round trip checks it, completeness finishes it, and even a whole parenthesized chunk can be the shared factor. Next, 2.4 adds and subtracts entire expressions, and the full sign flip from distributing \(-1\) in 2.1 becomes the main skill.

review.x: Compute \(34\cdot 67+34\cdot 33\) without working out either product. Factor out the shared \(34\) first and see what sum is left behind it.
hints: The sum left behind the \(34\) is \(67+33\), a friendly number.
review.sol: Grinding out \(2278+1122\) works, just slowly. Both terms contain \(34\), so the sum is \(34(67+33)=34\cdot 100=\boxed{3400}\).

review.x: Compute \(76\cdot 53-76\cdot 43\) by factoring first. Both products are ugly, but the difference left behind the shared factor is not.
hints: Pull the \(76\) out front and subtract inside the parentheses.
review.sol: Working out \(4028-3268\) gets there the slow way. The shared \(76\) comes out front instead, since \(76(53-43)=76\cdot 10=\boxed{760}\).

review.x: The expression \(5q+60\) factors as \(5(q+c)\) for one constant \(c\). Divide each term by the shared \(5\), then enter \(c\).
hints: Divide each term by \(5\), the second one included.
review.sol: Writing \(5(q+60)\) divides only the first term and fails the round trip. Since \(60\div 5=12\), the factoring is \(5(q+12)\), and expanding gives back \(5q+60\), so \(c=\boxed{12}\).

review.x: Enter the largest number that divides both terms of \(38x+95\). Neither number is small, so factor each one before searching for what they share.
hints: Both \(38\) and \(95\) are a prime times a small number.
review.sol: Testing small numbers stalls, since \(2\) misses \(95\) and \(5\) misses \(38\). Factoring instead, \(38=2\cdot 19\) and \(95=5\cdot 19\), so the complete form is \(19(2x+5)\) and the largest common factor is \(\boxed{19}\).

review.x: Ravi factors \(44h+66\) as \(11(4h+6)\), and the expansion checks out. Even so, \(4h\) and \(6\) still share a factor, so the factoring is not complete. Enter the largest common factor of the two original terms.
hints: Passing the round trip is not the same as being finished. Look at what \(4h\) and \(6\) still share.
review.sol: Accepting \(11\) because the expansion checks out is the trap, since the leftovers \(4h\) and \(6\) still share \(2\). The full factor is \(\gcd(44,66)=22\), the complete form \(22(2h+3)\) expands back to \(44h+66\), and the answer is \(\boxed{22}\).

review.x: The shared factor is a letter this time. Since \(n^2\) is \(n\cdot n\), both terms of \(n^2+13n\) contain \(n\), so the expression factors as \(n(n+c)\). Enter \(c\).
hints: Divide each term by \(n\).
review.sol: The terms share no number, but both contain \(n\). Dividing each by \(n\) leaves \(n+13\), and the round trip \(n(n+13)=n^2+13n\) confirms \(c=\boxed{13}\).

review.x: Number and letter both matter here. Enter the greatest common factor of the two terms of \(15u^2+25u\), typed like a term, for example \(8w\).
hints: The number part comes from \(15\) and \(25\), and the letter part from what both terms share.
review.sol: Entering only \(5\) or only \(u\) stops halfway. The number part is \(\gcd(15,25)=5\) and both terms contain \(u\), so the complete form is \(5u(3u+5)\) and the greatest common factor is \(\boxed{5u}\).

review.x: The expression \(26x+39y-52\) factors as \(13(2x+3y+e)\). Enter \(e\), sign included.
hints: Divide the last term by \(13\), sign and all.
review.sol: Dropping the minus and entering \(4\) is the trap. Since \(-52\div 13=-4\), the factoring is \(13(2x+3y-4)\), and expanding gives back \(26x+39y-52\), so \(e=\boxed{-4}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\dfrac{7\cdot 93+7\cdot 7}{10}\) without any long multiplication. The top is a sum of two products with a shared factor, so factor it before dividing anything.
hints: The top is \(7\) copies of a friendly sum.
review.sol: Multiplying out \(651+49\) reaches \(700\) the slow way. Factoring the top gives \(7(93+7)=7\cdot 100=700\), and \(700\div 10=\boxed{70}\).

review.x: Both terms of \(d(d+5)+3(d+5)\) contain the whole chunk \(d+5\), so it can come out front like any shared factor. Factoring it out gives \((d+a)(d+5)\). Enter \(a\).
hints: Count copies of \(d+5\), treating the chunk as a single object.
review.sol: The tempting answer is \(5\), since it is the other number in sight, but \(5\) sits inside the shared chunk \(d+5\), not in the leftover factor. Count copies of the chunk instead. The first term is \(d\) copies of \(d+5\) and the second is \(3\) more, so the expression is \(d+3\) copies of the chunk, \((d+3)(d+5)\). Distributing the chunk back over \(d+3\) reproduces the original, and \(a=\boxed{3}\).

review.x: One more full scan, this one on \(6t^2+9t\). Enter the greatest common factor of its two terms, typed like a term. Check that the leftovers share nothing before you commit.
hints: Find the number part first, then bring along the letter both terms share.
review.sol: Stopping at \(3\) leaves the shared \(t\) behind. Since \(\gcd(6,9)=3\) and both terms contain \(t\), the complete form is \(3t(2t+3)\), whose leftovers share only \(1\), so the greatest common factor is \(\boxed{3t}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(41^2+41\) by factoring before you compute anything. Done right, one clean product replaces a square plus an addition.
hints: Both terms contain \(41\), since \(41^2\) means \(41\cdot 41\).
After the \(41\) comes out, the parentheses hold \(41+1\).
review.sol: Squaring first gives \(1681+41=1722\) and works, but factoring is cleaner, \(41^2+41=41(41+1)=41\cdot 42=1722\). The shape is general, since \(n^2+n\) is always \(n(n+1)\), a product of two consecutive whole numbers, and the value is \(\boxed{1722}\).

blocks.x: 2.3 closed by saying this lesson would add and subtract entire expressions, with the sign flip from distributing \(-1\) as the main skill. Both halves come true. The starting point is smaller than that, plain counting, because adding expressions begins with counting how many copies of a letter you have.
Start by counting. Nine copies of \(k\) plus four more copies of \(k\) is some number of copies of \(k\), so \(9k+4k=ck\) for a single number \(c\). Enter \(c\).
hints: Count copies and ignore what one copy is worth. Nine of something plus four more of the same thing is how many of it?
blocks.sol: The reflex to resist is multiplying the two coefficients, since copies add here, they never multiply. Nine copies plus four more copies is thirteen copies, whatever a copy happens to be worth. Written out, \(9k+4k=13k\), so \(c=\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: Like terms. Terms with identical variable parts, like \(9k\) and \(4k\), are called like terms, and they combine by adding their coefficients. The reason comes straight from 2.3, and it fits in one line, $$9k+4k=(9+4)k=13k.$$ Combining like terms is factoring, the shared letter is the common factor and the coefficients add behind it, so nothing new is being invented in this lesson. The coefficients do all the arithmetic and the letter rides along unchanged.
Jae simplifies \(7w+3\) to \(10w\). Test the claim the 1.4 way, evaluate both expressions at \(w=2\), each on its own, and enter how far apart the two values are.
hints: One input, two values. Work out \(7w+3\) at \(w=2\), then \(10w\) at \(w=2\), and compare.
blocks.sol: The merge \(7+3=10\) treats \(7w\) and \(3\) as the same kind of term, and one evaluation exposes it. At \(w=2\) the original gives \(14+3=17\) while \(10w\) gives \(20\), and by 1.4 one input with two different outputs proves the claim false. The gap is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Unlike terms stay apart. Nothing was wrong with \(7w+3\) in the first place. Its terms share no factor to pull out, so no combining is possible and the expression was already finished. The split is not just letters against numbers, since 1.5 makes \(x^2\) mean \(x\cdot x\), a different factor structure from \(x\), so \(x^2\) and \(x\) are unlike as well. Simplified does not mean one term. It means every family has been collected exactly once.

blocks.cap: On top, adding sorts like with like. The two \(x\) tiles collect into one \(x\) tile and the two plain numbers collect into one number, and nothing crosses between the groups. Below, a minus in front of parentheses is a gate that multiplies every term inside by \(-1\), so both signs flip, and the grey route that flips only the first sign is the error to watch for.

blocks.x: Add two expressions. \((5d+9)+(2d+8)\) simplifies to one \(d\) term plus one constant. Enter the constant term of the result.
hints: In a pure sum the parentheses change nothing by 1.3, so drop them and collect each family on its own.
blocks.sol: Mashing all four numbers into one pile is the trap, since \(5d\) and \(9\) are unlike. Dropping the parentheses leaves \(5d+9+2d+8\), the \(d\) family collects to \(7d\) and the constants to \(9+8=17\), so the sum is \(7d+17\) and the constant term is \(\boxed{17}\).

blocks.x: Adding expressions. In a pure sum the parentheses do nothing, since 1.3 lets addition regroup and reorder freely, so \((5d+9)+(2d+8)\) is just the four terms \(5d+9+2d+8\) taken in whatever order helps. Drop the parentheses, then sweep family by family, every \(d\) term into one total, every constant into another, and write the totals side by side. Two families in, two terms out.
Now subtract. \((9p+7)-(4p+3)\) simplifies to one \(p\) term plus one constant. Enter the coefficient of \(p\) in the result.
hints: The minus applies to the whole second expression, so both \(4p\) and \(3\) get subtracted.
blocks.sol: Subtract family by family, and remember the minus applies to the whole second expression. The tempting slip is to flip only the first sign and write \(9p+7-4p+3=5p+10\), which adds the \(3\) instead of subtracting it. Done correctly, the \(p\) terms give \(9p-4p=5p\) and the constants give \(7-3=4\), so the result is \(5p+4\). The constant \(4\) answers a different question, the coefficient of \(p\) is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \((11h+9)-(7h-5)\). The expression being subtracted carries a minus of its own this time. Enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: Rewrite the subtraction first. Distribute \(-1\) over the whole second expression, the 2.1 move.
After the rewrite, check what happened to the \(-5\). What sign does it carry when the constants collect?
blocks.sol: Flipping only the first sign gives \(11h+9-7h-5\), with constants \(9-5=4\), and that is the classic error. The minus multiplies every term of \(7h-5\) by \(-1\), so the rewrite is \(11h+9-7h+5\), the \(h\) family gives \(4h\), and the constants give \(9+5=\boxed{14}\).

blocks.x: Subtracting is distributing \(-1\). A minus in front of parentheses is multiplication by \(-1\), the rule from 2.1, so subtracting an expression multiplies every one of its terms by \(-1\) and then everything adds. In practice, flip every sign of the expression being subtracted, write the flipped line down, then sweep family by family as usual. The most common simplification error in all of algebra flips only the first sign, and writing the fully flipped line before collecting anything is the habit that kills it.
Simplify \((7u+12)-(7u+4)\) completely, flipping both signs of the second expression first. The result is shorter than usual. Enter the single number that remains.
hints: Run the sweep and watch what the \(u\) family adds up to.
blocks.sol: Flipping both signs gives \(7u+12-7u-4\). If you flip only the first sign you get \(7u+12-7u+4\), which totals \(16\) and is wrong. Done correctly, the \(u\) family totals \(7u-7u=0u\), so the \(u\) terms drop out entirely, and the constants give \(12-4=8\). The whole expression simplifies to \(\boxed{8}\).

blocks.x: Families can vanish. A family can total zero copies and drop out of the answer entirely, the way \(7u-7u\) just did, leaving only a plain number behind. The constants can vanish the same way, leaving a pure variable term. Zero copies of anything is nothing, so write nothing, not \(0u\). A long expression collapsing to something tiny is not a mistake, it is exactly what the sweep is for, and it happens constantly once equations arrive in chapter 4.
Two letters now. Simplify \((4a+9b)+(6a-2b)\). The \(a\) terms and the \(b\) terms collect separately. Enter the coefficient of \(b\) in the result.
hints: Each letter is its own family, one sweep for the \(a\) terms and a separate sweep for the \(b\) terms.
blocks.sol: One sweep per letter. The \(a\) family gives \(4a+6a=10a\), the \(b\) family gives \(9b-2b=7b\), and the two totals never mix, so the result is \(10a+7b\). The \(10\) belongs to \(a\), and the coefficient of \(b\) is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: Three expressions chained together. Simplify \((2t-5)+(6-3t)+(4t+1)\). The constants sit in a different spot inside each pair of parentheses. Enter the constant term of the result.
hints: One sweep per family, and 1.3 says the order the terms arrive in does not matter.
blocks.sol: Position changes nothing, the sweep collects the constants from wherever they sit. The \(t\) family gives \(2t-3t+4t=3t\) and the constants give \(-5+6+1=2\), so the result is \(3t+2\). The \(3\) is the coefficient, not the ask, and the constant term is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: One sweep per family. However many expressions are chained together, one sweep per family finishes the job, and a written running total for each family, the 2.2 habit of putting middle steps on paper, is what keeps the signs honest. Two notes for the practice ahead. A family's total can come out negative, and that minus stays glued to the coefficient in the answer. And a bare letter counts as one copy, \(r\) means \(1r\), so it joins its family's count like any other term.
The whole chapter in one line. Expand \(k(k+9)\) with 2.1, then simplify \(k(k+9)-k^2\) completely. One family remains. Enter the coefficient of \(k\).
hints: Expand first. 2.1 turns \(k(k+9)\) into two terms.
After expanding, decide which terms are actually like. 1.5 says \(k^2\) and \(k\) are different families.
blocks.sol: Expanding gives \(k^2+9k-k^2\). Combining \(k^2\) with \(9k\) is the tempting move, but they are unlike by 1.5, so the only like pair is \(k^2-k^2\), which vanishes. What remains is \(9k\), and the coefficient of \(k\) is \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.label: Fun fact
blocks.x: Chapter 14 adds and subtracts entire polynomials, expressions carrying a dozen terms or more at once, and every bit of that work is this lesson's family-by-family sweep run at a bigger scale.

Expressions now add, subtract, expand, and factor, four moves resting on the same handful of chapter 1 rules, and all of it reduces to counting copies and flipping signs. Next, 2.5 turns to fractions whose tops and bottoms hold variables, and the same care with rewriting carries straight over.

review.x: Twelve copies of \(g\) plus six more copies is some number of copies of \(g\). Write \(12g+6g\) as a single term \(cg\) and enter \(c\).
hints: Count copies of \(g\), the way you would count any objects.
review.sol: Multiplying the coefficients would give \(72\), but addition counts copies rather than multiplying them. Twelve copies of \(g\) plus six more is eighteen copies, so \(12g+6g=18g\) and \(c=\boxed{18}\).

review.x: A student simplifies \(8y+1\) to \(9y\). Settle it the 1.4 way. Evaluate both expressions at \(y=5\), each on its own, and enter how far apart their values are.
hints: One input, two values. Substitute \(y=5\) into each expression separately.
review.sol: The merge \(8+1=9\) treats the unlike terms \(8y\) and \(1\) as like. At \(y=5\) the original gives \(40+1=41\) while \(9y\) gives \(45\), one mismatch proves the claim false, and the gap is \(45-41=\boxed{4}\).

review.x: Two expressions added. Simplify \((3c+10)+(8c+5)\) down to one \(c\) term plus one constant, and enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: In a pure sum the parentheses drop. Then collect family by family.
review.sol: The parentheses drop and the families collect, \(3c+8c=11c\) and \(10+5=15\). Entering \(11\) names the coefficient instead of the constant. The constant term is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: Now a subtraction. Simplify \((12v+11)-(8v+1)\) down to one \(v\) term plus one constant, and enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: The minus reaches both terms of \((8v+1)\), not just the \(8v\).
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign would leave \(+1\) and a constant of \(12\). The minus multiplies both terms, so the expression becomes \(12v+11-8v-1\) and the constants give \(11-1=\boxed{10}\).

review.x: Simplify \((9s+4)-(3s-7)\) down to one \(s\) term plus one constant, and enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: What does the minus in front of \((3s-7)\) do to the \(-7\)?
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign gives \(9s+4-3s-7\) and a constant of \(4-7=-3\). The minus multiplies every term of \((3s-7)\), so the \(-7\) becomes \(+7\) and the constant term is \(4+7=\boxed{11}\).

review.x: Simplify \((5x+20)-(5x+14)\) completely and enter the single number that remains. Distribute the minus first, then total each family.
hints: Watch what the \(x\) family adds up to before writing the result.
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign lands on \(20+14=34\). Distributed fully, the expression is \(5x+20-5x-14\), the \(x\) family totals \(5-5=0\) copies and vanishes, and zero copies of \(x\) is nothing, so all that remains is \(20-14=\boxed{6}\).

review.x: Two letters and a subtraction. Simplify \((5a+6b)-(3a+13b)\) and enter the coefficient of \(b\) in the simplified result, sign included.
hints: A family's total can come out negative. Track the sign of the \(b\) sweep.
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign leaves \(+13b\) and a \(b\) total of \(6+13=19\). Distributed fully, the \(b\) family is \(6b-13b=-7b\), and the minus stays glued to the coefficient, so the answer is \(\boxed{-7}\).

review.x: Three expressions chained. Simplify \((6n+11)+(n-15)+(2n+4)\) with one sweep per family, then enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: The bare \(n\) counts as one copy, \(1n\).
review.sol: In a pure sum every parenthesis drops, and the bare \(n\) counts as one copy, so the \(n\) family is \(6+1+2=9\). Entering \(9\) names the wrong part. The constants collect to \(11-15+4=\boxed{0}\).

review.x: Simplify \((3x^2+8x)+(9x^2-2x)\) and enter the coefficient of \(x^2\) in the simplified result. Keep each family separate as you collect.
hints: \(x^2\) and \(x\) are separate families by 1.5. Collect each on its own.
review.sol: Adding all four coefficients into one total, \(3+8+9-2=18\) treats \(x^2\) and \(x\) as one family, but their factor structures differ, so they collect separately. The result is \(12x^2+6x\), and the coefficient of \(x^2\) is \(3+9=\boxed{12}\).

review.x: Simplify first, then evaluate. Reduce \((6q+10)-(4q-3)\) to one \(q\) term plus one constant, then evaluate the simplified result at \(q=4\) and enter the value.
hints: Simplify before substituting. It turns the evaluation into one small step.
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign gives \(2q+7\), which evaluates to \(15\) at \(q=4\). Subtracting the whole quantity changes the sign of the \(-3\) as well, so the simplified form is \(2q+13\), and at \(q=4\) the value is \(8+13=\boxed{21}\).

review.x: Expand, then combine. Rewrite \(4(r+8)-r\) with the parentheses gone, collect the \(r\) family, and enter the constant term of the simplified result.
hints: Distribute the \(4\) over both inside terms before touching the \(-r\).
review.sol: Distributing onto the \(r\) alone gives \(4r+8-r\) and a constant of \(8\), the 2.1 partial-expansion error. The \(4\) multiplies both inside terms, so the expansion is \(4r+32-r=3r+32\) and the constant term is \(\boxed{32}\).

review.x: Three expressions, one subtraction. Simplify \((5z+16)+(7z-6)-(12z+9)\) completely and enter the single number that remains.
hints: Rewrite the subtraction by distributing the minus over both terms of \((12z+9)\) first.
Then run one sweep per family, the \(z\) terms in one pass and the constants in another.
review.sol: Flipping only the first sign of the last expression keeps the \(+9\) and gives a constant of \(16-6+9=19\). Distributed fully, the \(z\) family totals \(5+7-12=0\) and vanishes, the constants total \(16-6-9=1\), and a check at \(z=2\) gives \(26+8-33=1\), confirming the expression equals \(1\) no matter what \(z\) is. What remains is \(\boxed{1}\).
